eli3: Skewness measures whether a distribution has a longer tail on one side. A distribution is right-skewed (positive skew) if it has a long right tail — like income data where most people earn modestly but a few earn enormous amounts. It is left-skewed (negative skew) if the long tail is on the left.

For a right-skewed distribution: mean > median > mode. The mean gets pulled toward the long tail, while the mode is at the peak. This ordering is a quick diagnostic — if mean > median in your data, it is likely right-skewed.
Skewness is the third standardised central moment: γ₁ = E[(X−μ)³]/σ³. Positive γ₁ = right tail; negative γ₁ = left tail; γ₁ = 0 = symmetric. The normal distribution has skewness 0.

whenWhy.title: Checking normality assumptions
whenWhy.body: Many statistical tests (t-test, ANOVA, regression) assume normality of errors. Skewness > |1| is a warning sign. A skewness test (D'Agostino-Pearson, Jarque-Bera) quantifies departure from normality. For ML, skewed features can hurt gradient-based models — log or Box-Cox transformation can reduce skewness.

whenWhy.title: Choosing the right central tendency measure
whenWhy.body: For right-skewed data (income, housing prices, survival times), mean > median — the mean overstates the 'typical' value. Communicate median for skewed data. US Census reports median income precisely because the income distribution is highly right-skewed.

whenWhy.title: Financial risk and return distributions
whenWhy.body: Equity returns exhibit negative skewness (fat left tail, rare but severe crashes). Option pricing and risk models must account for skewness — models assuming normal returns (zero skewness) underestimate tail risk. The Sortino ratio, downside deviation, and CVaR are measures designed for negatively skewed return distributions.

whenWhy.title: Log transformation for skewed data
whenWhy.body: For right-skewed, positive-valued data, log(x) often produces approximate normality. This is because the log-normal distribution (common in nature due to multiplicative effects) has exactly zero skewness on the log scale. Box-Cox transformation generalises this: x^λ (or log x when λ→0).

deepDiveIntro: Population skewness is the expected value of the cubed standardized deviation — the third standardized central moment. The sample version adjusts the raw moment estimate with a correction factor to reduce bias. A simpler biased estimator divides the third central moment by the cube of the standard deviation.

Positive skewness indicates a longer right tail and corresponds to a mean that exceeds the median. Negative skewness indicates a longer left tail and a mean below the median. Zero skewness means the distribution is symmetric, though not necessarily normal. Values with absolute skewness below 0.5 are roughly symmetric; above 1 is considered highly skewed.

deepDive.heading: Definition: Third Standardised Moment
deepDive.body: Skewness measures asymmetry by checking whether one tail stretches farther than the other. Positive values indicate a longer right tail, negative values indicate a longer left tail, and values near zero suggest symmetry. Because the statistic is standardized, it can be compared across variables measured on very different scales.

deepDive.heading: Named Distributions and Their Skewness
deepDive.body: Different distributions have characteristic skewness patterns. Symmetric families such as the normal have zero skewness, while distributions like the exponential and log-normal are inherently right-skewed. This makes skewness a quick diagnostic for whether a familiar distributional assumption is even plausible.

deepDive.heading: Pearson's Skewness Coefficients
deepDive.body: Pearson proposed simpler skewness summaries based on the relative positions of the mean, median, and mode. These are easier to interpret than third-moment formulas and are useful as descriptive heuristics, especially when you want a quick sense of asymmetry without relying on higher-order moments. They are less general, but often more intuitive.

deepDive.heading: Jarque-Bera Test for Normality
deepDive.body: Skewness is often paired with kurtosis in formal tests of normality. The Jarque-Bera test asks whether the sample looks too asymmetric or too heavy-tailed to be consistent with a normal distribution. It is a compact way to combine two common shape diagnostics into one test statistic.

takeaways: Skewness γ₁ = E[(X−μ)³]/σ³: 3rd standardised central moment; 0 = symmetric, >0 = right tail, <0 = left tail. [1]
Right-skewed: mean > median > mode; left-skewed: mean < median < mode. [1]
Sample estimator: ĝ₁ = [n/((n−1)(n−2))] Σ[(xᵢ−x̄)/s]³ (Fisher's adjusted, approximately unbiased). [1]
Jarque-Bera test combines skewness and kurtosis: JB ~ χ²(2) under normality. [2]
Log transformation reduces right-skewness; Box-Cox generalises to any power transformation. [1]
Financial returns often show negative skewness (crash risk in left tail). [1]

edgeCases.q: Can a distribution be symmetric but not normal?
edgeCases.a: Yes. Symmetry (γ₁ = 0) is necessary but not sufficient for normality. The Student-t, uniform, and Laplace distributions are all symmetric but not normal. Normality requires additionally that all higher moments match those of the normal. The Jarque-Bera test checks both skewness and excess kurtosis; passing both is still not definitive (only indicative). [1]

edgeCases.q: Why is skewness sensitive to outliers?
edgeCases.a: Skewness uses the third power of deviations: (xᵢ−x̄)³. A single observation far from the mean contributes proportionally to its cube — outliers dominate the sample skewness estimate. This is why the sample skewness can give misleading results for small samples with heavy tails. Robust alternatives include Groeneveld-Meeden skewness based on the median and quartiles: GM = (mean−median)/MAD. [1]

sources.label: Casella & Berger — Statistical Inference (2nd ed.), Ch. 2
Jarque & Bera (1980) — Efficient Tests for Normality, Econometrica Letters 6(3)
